structured.summary: This paper proposes lightweight and accurate out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods for conditional language models (CLMs) using input and output embeddings, demonstrating effectiveness on abstractive summarization and translation tasks. It further introduces selective generation by combining OOD scores with perplexity to abstain from low-quality outputs under distribution shift, supported by human evaluations and extensive experiments.

strengths.point: Novel OOD detection method leveraging input and output embeddings with Mahalanobis distance and Relative Mahalanobis Distance (RMD) tailored for CLMs.
strengths.evidence: Section 2.2 details the embedding-based OOD detection approach and its extension of RMD to conditional language models.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation on two important conditional language generation tasks: abstractive summarization and machine translation.
strengths.evidence: Section 3 describes experiments on PEGASUS for summarization and Transformer for translation with multiple in-domain and OOD datasets.

strengths.point: Demonstration that perplexity alone is insufficient for OOD detection and quality estimation, and that combining OOD scores with perplexity improves selective generation.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.2 and 4.3 show perplexity's limitations and the benefits of combined scores for quality prediction and selective generation.

strengths.point: Use of human evaluation to validate summarization quality and a novel Quality vs Abstention curve to assess selective generation performance.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.1 and 4.4 describe human ratings collected via Mechanical Turk and the QA curve evaluation metric.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of decoder-only architectures, which are prevalent in large language models.
weaknesses.evidence: The method is demonstrated only on encoder-decoder Transformers; Section 6 mentions expectation but no experiments on decoder-only models.

weaknesses.point: The approach relies on fitting Gaussian distributions to embeddings, which may not capture complex multimodal embedding distributions.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2 fits single Gaussians to input and output embeddings; no discussion of limitations or alternatives for non-Gaussian distributions.

weaknesses.point: Background distributions are fitted using large datasets (C4, ParaCrawl) with decoded outputs, which may introduce noise or bias in background modeling.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2 describes fitting background output Gaussian using decoded outputs from large datasets without ground-truth.

weaknesses.point: The binary classifier baseline is logistic regression on embeddings, which may be too simple compared to more complex discriminative models.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2 and experiments use logistic regression as a binary classifier without exploring more powerful classifiers.

weaknesses.point: Selective generation evaluation focuses on abstaining from low-quality outputs but does not explore downstream user impact or deployment scenarios in detail.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4 discusses selective generation and abstention but lacks analysis of practical deployment or user experience.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not provide ablation studies isolating the contributions of input vs output embeddings or the effect of covariance estimation choices.
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments report combined results but do not separately analyze input-only or output-only embeddings or covariance modeling.

weaknesses.point: The method assumes availability of large background datasets representative of general domains, which may not be feasible in all applications.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2 relies on C4 and ParaCrawl for background Gaussian fitting; no discussion on applicability when such data is unavailable.

weaknesses.point: Human evaluation is limited to summarization and a relatively small sample size (100 examples per dataset), which may limit generalizability.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 and Appendix A.3 describe human ratings on 100 examples per dataset.

questions.question: How does the proposed OOD detection method perform on decoder-only language models such as GPT-style architectures?
questions.motivation: Decoder-only models are widely used in practice; evaluating applicability would broaden impact.

questions.question: Have the authors considered modeling embedding distributions with more flexible models (e.g., mixture models) to better capture complex data distributions?
questions.motivation: Single Gaussian assumptions may limit detection accuracy in complex embedding spaces.

questions.question: Can the authors provide more details or experiments on the sensitivity of the method to the choice and quality of the background dataset used for fitting background Gaussians?
questions.motivation: Background modeling is critical for RMD; understanding robustness to background data choice is important.

questions.question: Is it possible to extend the selective generation framework to provide calibrated confidence scores or uncertainty estimates for generated outputs?
questions.motivation: Confidence estimates would be valuable for downstream applications and user trust.

critique_points.point_001: Limited exploration of decoder-only architectures, which are prevalent in large language models. (The method is demonstrated only on encoder-decoder Transformers; Section 6 mentions expectation but no experiments on decoder-only models.)
critique_points.point_002: The approach relies on fitting Gaussian distributions to embeddings, which may not capture complex multimodal embedding distributions. (Section 2.2 fits single Gaussians to input and output embeddings; no discussion of limitations or alternatives for non-Gaussian distributions.)
critique_points.point_003: Background distributions are fitted using large datasets (C4, ParaCrawl) with decoded outputs, which may introduce noise or bias in background modeling. (Section 2.2 describes fitting background output Gaussian using decoded outputs from large datasets without ground-truth.)
critique_points.point_004: The binary classifier baseline is logistic regression on embeddings, which may be too simple compared to more complex discriminative models. (Section 2.2 and experiments use logistic regression as a binary classifier without exploring more powerful classifiers.)
critique_points.point_005: Selective generation evaluation focuses on abstaining from low-quality outputs but does not explore downstream user impact or deployment scenarios in detail. (Section 4 discusses selective generation and abstention but lacks analysis of practical deployment or user experience.)
critique_points.point_006: The paper does not provide ablation studies isolating the contributions of input vs output embeddings or the effect of covariance estimation choices. (Experiments report combined results but do not separately analyze input-only or output-only embeddings or covariance modeling.)
critique_points.point_007: The method assumes availability of large background datasets representative of general domains, which may not be feasible in all applications. (Section 2.2 relies on C4 and ParaCrawl for background Gaussian fitting; no discussion on applicability when such data is unavailable.)
critique_points.point_008: Human evaluation is limited to summarization and a relatively small sample size (100 examples per dataset), which may limit generalizability. (Section 4.1 and Appendix A.3 describe human ratings on 100 examples per dataset.)
critique_points.point_009: How does the proposed OOD detection method perform on decoder-only language models such as GPT-style architectures? (Decoder-only models are widely used in practice; evaluating applicability would broaden impact.)
critique_points.point_010: Have the authors considered modeling embedding distributions with more flexible models (e.g., mixture models) to better capture complex data distributions? (Single Gaussian assumptions may limit detection accuracy in complex embedding spaces.)
critique_points.point_011: Can the authors provide more details or experiments on the sensitivity of the method to the choice and quality of the background dataset used for fitting background Gaussians? (Background modeling is critical for RMD; understanding robustness to background data choice is important.)
critique_points.point_012: Is it possible to extend the selective generation framework to provide calibrated confidence scores or uncertainty estimates for generated outputs? (Confidence estimates would be valuable for downstream applications and user trust.)
